hero.subtitle: Frictionless story point estimation with a friendly Corgi twist. No registration, no paywalls, just effective (and fun!) planning.

whatIs.heading: What is Planning Poker?
whatIs.p1: Planning poker is a consensus-based agile estimation technique used by scrum teams during sprint planning. Each team member privately selects a card representing their estimate for a user story or task — typically using a Fibonacci-like sequence (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13…) or T-shirt sizes (XS, S, M, L, XL). Cards are revealed simultaneously to prevent anchoring bias, then the team discusses any large discrepancies before reaching a shared estimate.
whatIs.p2: The technique was introduced by James Grenning in 2002 and popularised by Mike Cohn in <book>Agile Estimating and Planning</book> (2005). It has since become the de-facto standard for story point estimation across scrum, kanban, and SAFe teams worldwide.

howItWorks.heading: How It Works
howItWorks.step1Desc: Click "Create Room", give it a name, and choose your card deck. You get a shareable link instantly — no account needed.
howItWorks.step2Desc: Share the room link with your colleagues. Anyone with the link can join from any browser or device.
howItWorks.step3Desc: Each participant picks a card privately. Reveal all cards at once, discuss outliers, and lock in your estimate.

why.heading: Why Use Corgi Planning Poker?
why.freeDesc: No subscription, no trial, no credit card required.
why.noRegDesc: Start estimating in under 30 seconds. Just create a room and share the link.
why.realtimeDesc: All votes update live across every participant's screen without refreshing.
why.customDesc: Use the default Fibonacci deck, T-shirt sizes, or create your own custom values.
why.crossPlatformDesc: Sign in with Google to link your identity across devices. Resume any room from your phone, tablet, or laptop — your history follows you.
why.spectatorDesc: Join a room as a silent observer without casting votes. Sit down at any time when you're ready to participate.
why.openSourceDesc: The full source code is publicly available on GitHub. Found a bug? Open an issue. Want to improve the app? Submit a pull request.
why.interactiveDesc: Keep the energy high during long planning sessions! Throw emojis at your teammates to celebrate agreement or lighten the mood.
why.jiraDesc: Connect your Jira workspace and link tickets directly to your estimation round. Browse open issues, see the title and link in-room, and push the agreed story points back to Jira in one click.

jira.heading: Jira Integration
jira.subtitle: Link Jira tickets to your estimation room. The whole team sees the issue title and link — then push the consensus story points straight back to Jira when you're done.
jira.step1Desc: Authorize once via Atlassian OAuth. Your token is stored server-side and shared across the room.
jira.step2Desc: Search any open Jira issue by key or title. The selected ticket syncs to all participants in real time.
jira.step3Desc: After revealing cards, push the consensus estimate back to the ticket's Story Points field with one click.
jira.mockTitle: As a user, I want to reset my password so that I can regain access to my account

story.heading: The Story Behind Corgi Planning Poker
story.p1: Like many agile teams, I started out using whatever free planning poker tools I could find. They worked well... until they didn't. One by one, the features I relied on started moving behind paywalls: session limits, room caps, and even the ability to customize your own point values. For something that's supposed to be lightweight and collaborative, it felt like the wrong direction.
story.p2: So, I decided to build my own. I wanted a tool that stays free forever, has no artificial limits, and gives teams full control over their card decks. The result is the app you're using right now.
story.p3: The corgi theme is purely personal. I have a corgi at home named <kimi>Kimi</kimi>, and naming a side project after your favorite pup is basically a law of software engineering. Corgi Planning Poker started as a small tool for my own team and has just kept growing from there.
story.support: This app will always be free. But if it makes your planning sessions a little more joyful, I'd love it if you'd <link>{supportLink}</link> to reward the real boss (the corgi) behind the scenes!

faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: How many people can join a room?
faq.a1: There is no hard limit. Rooms work well for teams of 3 to 20 people. For very large groups, consider splitting into smaller estimation sub-teams.
faq.q2: Do I need to create an account?
faq.a2: No account is required. Every visitor is automatically assigned a unique guest ID so you can create and join rooms instantly. Signing in with Google links that ID to your Google account, which syncs your identity across browsers and devices — so you can resume any recent room from your phone, laptop, or any other device without losing your history. In our experience, removing this barrier is the single most impactful change for teams adopting a new estimation tool.
faq.a3: Four preset decks are built in: Fibonacci (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, 34), T-Shirt (XS, S, M, L, XL, XXL), Powers of 2 (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64), and Hours (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 24, 40). You can also create a fully custom deck by entering any comma-separated values. Mark any deck as a favourite and it will be auto-selected the next time you create a room.
faq.q4: Can I watch a session without voting?
faq.a4: Yes. When joining a room you can choose "Watch as spectator" to observe without casting a vote. You can sit down and join the voting at any point during the session.
faq.q5: How long does a room last?
faq.a5: Rooms are automatically removed after 30 days of inactivity. As long as your team uses a room at least once a month, it stays available.
faq.a6: Yes, completely free — no paid plans, no advertisements, no data sold, ever. If it saves your team time each sprint, consider buying Corgi a treat via Ko-fi or GitHub Sponsors. It helps keep the project alive and the corgi fed.
faq.q7: What is the difference between story points and hours?
faq.a7: Story points measure relative effort and complexity rather than clock time. A 5-point story is roughly twice as complex as a 2-point story for your team, but the actual hours vary by person. Using story points removes pressure to commit to specific durations and focuses the conversation on scope and risk instead.

openSource.heading: Free & Open Source
openSource.bugDesc: Something not working as expected? Open an issue on GitHub and we'll look into it.
openSource.contributeDesc: The codebase is open on GitHub. Pull requests are welcome — check the repo for guidelines.
openSource.supportDesc: This will always be free. But if it saves your team time each sprint, buying Corgi a treat means a lot.
